channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Obamacare Enrollment Drops Sharply as Costs Rise
item.description: Americans can’t afford the higher health insurance premiums that resulted from Congress’s refusal to extend federal tax credits.
item.dc:creator: Reed Abelson and Margot Sanger-Katz
item.category: Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act (2010)
Blue Cross and Blue Shield Assn
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Although the government has not yet reported enrollment numbers, insurers and analysts are estimating overall declines of about 20 percent compared with last year.

item.title: The 60-Day Deadline for the War in Iran, and an End to the D.H.S. Shutdown
item.description: Plus, your Friday news quiz.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cleaning up rubble in an apartment in Tehran that U.S.-Israeli airstrikes hit in March.

item.title: Trump’s Plans to Boost Weapons Production Might Not Deliver for Years
item.description: While the defense industry has announced plans to make more munitions, much of that expanded production will not quickly kick in.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Andrew Caballero-Reynolds/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The administration does not yet have the funding or congressional support for the weapons it needs for the possible future wars.

item.title: As Israel Entrenches, Frustration With Hezbollah Turns to Support
item.description: With the cease-fire fraying and Israel demolishing villages in the south, many Hezbollah supporters in Lebanon are putting aside their annoyance with the group and turning to it for protection.
item.dc:creator: Christina Goldbaum and David Guttenfelder
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: David Guttenfelder/The New York Times

item.title: Hezbollah Using Fiber-Optic Drones Against Israeli Targets
item.description: The Lebanese militant group is attacking Israeli troops with explosive drones controlled by fiber-optic cables, like those commonly used in the war in Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner, Natan Odenheimer, Aaron Boxerman and Adam Rasgon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Shir Torem/Reuters
item.media:description: An Israeli military artillery unit on the Israeli side of the border with Lebanon on Thursday.

item.title: Billionaire Chris Larsen Plans to Spend $3.5 Million in NY House Race Amid Midterm Clash Over A.I.
item.description: Chris Larsen, who hails from California, plans to spend $3.5 million to help Alex Bores, a New York congressional candidate at the center of a proxy war over A.I. regulation.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher and Nicholas Fandos
item.media:credit: Cayce Clifford for The New York Times
item.media:description: The tech billionaire Chris Larsen said that attacks on Alex Bores, a House candidate in New York, by a super PAC aligned with OpenAI were “really despicable.”

item.title: Republicans Want Tennessee’s Last Democratic House District
item.description: Republicans sliced Nashville into three G.O.P.-leaning congressional districts in 2022. After the Supreme Court decision on voting rights, Memphis could be next.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Andrew Harnik/Getty Images
item.media:description: Senator Marsha Blackburn, the favorite to become the state’s next governor, urged the state legislature to quickly adopt a new congressional map putting parts of Memphis, a majority Black city, in Republican hands.

item.title: Supreme Court Voting Rights Ruling Could Fuel New Era of Redistricting Wars
item.description: The expected flood of new congressional maps is likely to produce fewer competitive districts, fewer ways for voters to hold elected officials accountable and more polarized politics.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s ruling on the Voting Rights Act could unleash another redistricting arms race.

item.title: What to Know About the Maine Senate Race After Janet Mills Drops Out
item.description: The exit of Gov. Janet Mills kicked off the general election early in what is likely to be one of the most important, expensive and combative Senate races of 2026.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times; Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Susan Collins of Maine, a Republican seen as vulnerable given her state’s leftward tilt, is set to face Graham Platner, a progressive oysterman and political newcomer.

item.title: Why Mayor Mamdani Didn’t Roll Out the Red Carpet for the Royals
item.description: Zohran Mamdani studiously avoided the type of red-carpet welcome most mayors would prepare for a royal visit, reflecting his upbringing but also drawing some criticism.
item.dc:creator: Emma Goldberg
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Former Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg, left, escorted King Charles III and Queen Camilla at the 9/11 Memorial, rather than New York City’s current mayor, Zohran Mamdani.

item.title: Mamdani Suggested UK Should Return the Koh-i-Noor Diamond. Here’s the Back Story.
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York suggested that Britain should return the gem, a symbol of colonial plunder. Here’s the back story of the famed jewel.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon and Hari Kumar
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Tim Graham Photo Library, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The Koh-i-Noor diamond, right of center, is set in a crown made for the British royal family.

item.title: What the End of Saudi Arabia’s Big-Spending Era Means for the Kingdom and Beyond
item.description: The kingdom is pulling back from a costly and high-profile golf venture amid mounting financial concerns, raising questions about its plans.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: A picture of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, in 2022.

item.title: Jared Kushner, Steve Witkoff and the Profitable Business of Peace
item.description: The Trump administration is turning diplomacy into an asset to be leveraged.
item.dc:creator: Linda Kinstler
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Stocks and Oil Prices Sent Conflicting Signals in April Amid Havoc of Iran War
item.description: Energy markets remained disrupted by turmoil in the Middle East, while the S&P 500 posted its best month since November 2020.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: The World’s Central Banks Are Wrestling With a Gigantic Problem
item.description: Inflationary surges and economic slowdowns set off by the war in Iran, tariffs and other Trump policies have forced monetary officials to adjust, our columnist says.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Federal Reserve said on Wednesday that it needed to hold rates steady because “developments in the Middle East are contributing to a high level of uncertainty about the economic outlook.”

item.title: Where in the World Is All That Gold Stored?
item.description: As central banks buy more gold, where to put all that heavy metal is an increasingly important question. Reserves must be secure and ready to trade in a crisis.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Federal Reserve Bank of New York
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: More than 60 central banks store gold at the Bank of England.

item.title: Data Centers: The Issue Uniting Liberals and Conservatives
item.description: Americans have soured on data centers, polls show, and the sentiment is profoundly bipartisan. How that will change our politics?
item.dc:creator: Sabrina Tavernise

item.title: Top Psychiatrists Call for a Greater Focus on Ceasing Medication
item.description: With Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. aiming to rein in the use of psychiatric drugs, psychiatrists are preemptively addressing how and when patients should quit taking them.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.media:credit: Colin Temple/Alamy

item.title: Jeffrey Epstein’s Possible Suicide Note Hidden From Public View
item.description: An inmate said he discovered the note after Mr. Epstein was found injured in his jail cell, weeks before his death. It’s now locked in a courthouse.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser, Steve Eder and Jan Ransom

item.title: Comey Indictment Shows Justice Dept. Got the Message From Bondi’s Firing
item.description: In naming only an interim successor as acting attorney general, President Trump has established even greater incentives to execute his most extreme demands, current and former officials say.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush
item.category: Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard/Reuters
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, who is seeking to become the permanent attorney general, is currently examining a range of other potential charges against James B. Comey.

item.title: Pentagon Makes Deals With A.I. Companies to Expand Classified Work
item.description: The agreements with six technology companies come amid the Defense Department’s dispute with Anthropic.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Sheera Frenkel
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic and the Pentagon have been locked in a debate over whether the company’s A.I. model could be used to pilot autonomous drones or work on domestic surveillance.

item.title: Trump’s Tariff Reversal Reopens a Major Market for Scotch Whisky
item.description: A 10 percent tax on American imports was hurting British sales, even though exporters had increased sales in India, China and other countries.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Emily Whitney for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Scottish whisky industry said it had been losing millions of pounds a week because of the American import tax.

item.title: Nile Rodgers Explains How He Wrote ‘We Are Family,’ ‘Le Freak’ and More
item.description: The co-founder of Chic, the de facto house band of New York’s late-70s disco boom, talks about taking inspiration from the city’s club scene.

item.title: How Taylor Swift Writes a Song, In Her Own Words
item.description: The artist shares stories behind some of her biggest hits, her love of a “rant bridge” and how life in the public eye informs the stories she tells in her songs.

item.title: As Kentucky Derby Becomes a Bigger Business, Locals Feel Left Behind
item.description: Locals say rising ticket prices and a late start time for the Oaks race on Friday are hurting residents and restaurants. “The only thing I see is greed,” one steakhouse owner said.
item.dc:creator: Joe Drape
item.media:credit: Luke Sharrett for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pat Francis, whose family has owned Pat’s Steakhouse since 1958, is closing his doors on both Friday and Saturday to protest how Churchill Downs Inc. has transformed the Kentucky Derby business.

item.title: In a Small Iowa Town, a Solution to a National Crisis
item.description: As the Trump administration guts weather agencies, a state’s low-cost flood tracking system offers a model for responding to deadly inundations.
item.dc:creator: The Editorial Board and Jillian Weinberger
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; photo by Justin Sullivan/Getty

item.title: Don’t Underestimate the Fury of Democratic Voters
item.description: A Democratic vision of the Tea Party is emerging.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg
item.media:credit: Mark Peterson for The New York Times

item.title: The Voting Rights Act
item.description: We explain the significance of the Supreme Court’s decision.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times

item.title: Seth Meyers Gives Trump an Earful About Ear Jokes
item.description: The “Late Night” host expressed secondhand embarrassment after the president referred to the “beautiful ears” and “super hearing” of a NASA official.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Lithuania Says It Broke Up Russian Sabotage and Murder Plots
item.description: The arrests of nine people are reminders of the threat Russia poses in Europe at a time when Washington has shifted focus to the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Michael Schwirtz and Tomas Dapkus
item.media:credit: Thierry Monasse/Getty Images
item.media:description: Ruslan Gabbasov, who Lithuania says was the target of a Russian murder conspiracy, in Brussels in 2023.

item.title: In Bulgaria, a New Government Challenges an Old Puppet Master
item.description: Delyan Peevski was a focus of protests that led to Bulgarian elections. Can a new government shake his hold on power?
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall
item.category: Movement for Rights and Freedoms (Bulgarian Political Party)
item.media:credit: Nikolay Doychinov/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Delyan Peevski greeting supporters in Sofia, Bulgaria, in 2024. To his critics, Mr. Peevski has come to embody Bulgaria’s failure to establish the rule of law.

item.title: Why So Many People Already Own Shares of Elon Musk’s SpaceX
item.description: Even before the rocket company holds a major initial public offering, many people own stock in it through so-called special purpose vehicles.
item.dc:creator: Erin Griffith and Ryan Mac
item.media:credit: Brandon Celi

item.title: What We Know About New York’s Proposed Tax Increases
item.description: City leaders and state lawmakers are pushing Gov. Kathy Hochul to raise taxes on the wealthy, and they have floated several options.
item.dc:creator: Grace Ashford
item.media:credit: José A. Alvarado Jr. for The New York Times
item.media:description: The City Council speaker, Julie Menin, and Mayor Zohran Mamdani appeared together to ask Gov. Kathy Hochul to consider adjusting a state tax that applies to business owners.
